claims: Erasmus et al. (2021) claim that the ambiguity of the term “explanation” in explainable AI can be resolved by adopting any of four extant accounts of explanation in the philosophy of science: Deductive Nomological, Inductive Statistical, Causal Mechanical, and New Mechanist models.

Erasmus et al. (2021) propose the “indefeasibility thesis” stating that explanations are invariant with respect to the complexity of both the explanans and the explanandum, implying that deep neural networks are scientifically explainable regardless of their complexity.

Páez (2024) argues that applying the four philosophical accounts of explanation to deep neural networks as if they were natural phenomena is mistaken because deep neural networks lack the general law‑like structure required by those accounts.

Rudin (2019) asserts that surrogate models used as explanations for black‑box machine learning systems are always false, because a perfectly faithful explanation would be identical to the original model and thus unnecessary.
Slack et al. (2021) demonstrate that counterfactual explanations can be easily manipulated, showing a lack of robustness in counterfactual XAI methods.

Keane et al. (2021) report that only 31 % (36 out of 117) of research papers evaluating counterfactual explanations in XAI included user studies, indicating limited empirical assessment of user understanding.

Alqaraawi et al. (2020) find that participants given saliency‑map explanations generated by layer‑wise relevance propagation for a convolutional neural network provided very limited help in anticipating the network’s output for new images.

Letham et al. (2015) introduce sparse decision‑list classifiers for stroke‑risk prediction that achieve comparable predictive performance to support vector machines while maintaining interpretability comparable to medical scoring systems.

The authors introduced a novel causality measure based on Lempel‑Ziv (LZ) complexity that evaluates the direction of causation between two univariate sequences by comparing the LZ‑P penalties of each direction.

The authors evaluated the proposed LZ‑based causality measure on coupled autoregressive (AR) processes of orders 1, 5, 20, and 100 and found that the measure correctly identified Y → X as the causal direction across varying coupling coefficients.

The authors applied the proposed LZ‑based causality measure to coupled logistic maps with coupling coefficient η up to 0.4 and reported correct identification of the causal direction, after which the LZ penalties became similar due to synchronization.

The authors tested the LZ‑based causality measure on 104 Tübingen cause‑effect pairs and obtained a macro F1‑score of 0.44 and an accuracy of 50.8 %, which is lower than the ≈58 % accuracy reported for ORIGO on a selected subset of 95 pairs.

The authors integrated the LZ‑based causality measure as a splitting criterion in decision trees and reported that on the synthetic AR dataset the LZ‑causal decision tree achieved a macro F1‑score 60.5 % higher than both the LZ‑distance decision tree and the Gini‑impurity decision tree.

The authors reported that on the UCI Heart Disease dataset the causal strength ranking produced by the LZ‑causal decision tree assigned the highest causal strength (0.4361) to the feature “Serum Cholesterol in mg/dl”.
The authors provided a Python implementation of their methods in a public GitHub repository at https://github.com/i-to-the-power-i/causal-lz-p-decision-tree.

The Iris dataset, hosted by the UCI Machine Learning Repository, consists of 150 instances, three classes, and four numeric features describing sepal length, sepal width, petal length, and petal width.

The GPO-VAE model achieved an Average Treatment Effect Pearson correlation (ATE‑ρ) of 0.7658 ± 0.00, an ATE‑R² of 0.5699 ± 0.01, and a Jaccard similarity of 0.3220 ± 0.01 on the Replogle K562 perturbation‑response dataset, outperforming all baseline VAEs listed in Table 3.

The GPO-VAE model achieved a mean Wasserstein distance (µWD) of 0.248 ± 0.012 and a false omission rate (FOR) of 0.022 ± 0.007 on the Replogle K562 gene‑regulatory‑network inference task, which were the best µWD and FOR values among all methods evaluated in Table 4.

The GRN inferred by GPO-VAE contained subnetworks that connect perturbed genes to the cancer‑associated proteins KRAS, MYC, and NTRK1, and these subnetworks were reported to be consistent with experimentally validated pathways in the literature (Figure 4).

The GPO-VAE model predicted post‑perturbation gene‑expression for the unseen perturbations TWISTNB, RPL26, and RPL34 with ATE‑ρ values of 0.792, 0.886, and 0.919 respectively, and corresponding ATE‑R² values of 0.578, 0.775, and 0.831 (Figure 5).

The latent perturbation encoder of GPO-VAE parameterizes a square matrix \(\hat{W}\) whose entries represent causal probabilities from source gene i to target gene j, thereby aligning the latent space with a gene‑regulatory‑network adjacency matrix.

The GPO loss objective of GPO-VAE combines a differential‑gene‑expression loss, a K‑hop accumulation loss with K = 5, and a sparsity penalty derived from the NOTEARS algorithm, and optimizes the parameters of \(\hat{W}\) toward biologically explainable GRNs (Equations 15‑20).

The GPO-VAE model incorporates counterfactual reasoning for artifact disentanglement by training a latent artifact encoder on quality‑control (QC) labels, following the methodology of CRADLE‑VAE (Section 2.2.1, Equation 4).

The GPO-VAE model was trained on the Replogle K562 dataset using the Adam optimizer for 2,000 epochs, a batch size of 512, a learning rate of 0.0003, a latent dimension of 1,546, a mask‑prior probability of 0.3, and an embedding‑prior scale of 1 (Section F.1).

The authors systematically tested the identifiability of both where‑then‑what and what‑then‑where mechanistic interpretability strategies by training small multi‑layer perceptrons on Boolean function tasks and exhaustively enumerating all candidate circuits and algorithmic mappings.

The authors found overwhelming evidence of non‑identifiability: multiple circuits perfectly replicated model behavior, multiple interpretations existed for a given circuit, several algorithms were perfectly causally aligned with the neural computation, and a single algorithm was causally aligned with different subspaces of the network.

In the XOR example, the authors identified 85 unique circuits with zero circuit error and 25 unique Boolean‑circuit abstractions that exactly matched the neural activations under the circuit‑first approach.

In the XOR example, the authors discovered 159 perfect minimal mappings for two candidate Boolean algorithms, demonstrating that both the algorithm (what) and its localization (where) are not unique under the what‑then‑where approach.

When the hidden‑layer size k was increased from 2 to 5, the median number of computational abstractions found by the circuit‑first method rose from 38 to 910,000 and the median number found by the algorithm‑first method rose from 8 to 3,700.

When the network was trained on multiple logic‑gate tasks (1 to 6 gates), the authors observed a statistically significant decrease in the number of interpretations per circuit up to four tasks (p = 0.05), after which the variation was not significant.

When Gaussian noise with standard deviation 0.1 was added to inputs during training, the authors reported no significant change in the number of algorithms or minimal mappings (p > 0.6) but a significant reduction in the number of circuits (p < 0.001) and a significant increase in interpretations per circuit (p < 0.001).
The authors made the code and model parameters used for all experiments publicly available on GitHub.
The authors acknowledge that the concept of the counterfactual was first formally introduced by philosopher David Lewis in 1973.
The authors state that statistician Donald Rubin introduced the potential outcomes framework in 1974.
The authors note that Judea Pearl’s structural causal model (SCM) approach derives counterfactuals from a functional causal model, as described in Pearl (2009).
The authors report that Martens and Provost first proposed counterfactual explanations for document classification in 2014.
The authors indicate that Wachter, Mittelstadt, and Russell generalized counterfactual explanations to tabular data in 2017.
The authors define a counterfactual as an IF‑THEN statement where the IF portion (treatment or input values) is unrealized and the THEN portion describes the potential outcome.
The authors describe the average treatment effect (ATE) as the expected difference E[Y(1) − Y(0)], a standard causal estimand.
The authors list the four core assumptions of Rubin’s potential outcomes framework: consistency, no interference between units (SUTVA), conditional unconfoundedness, and positivity/overlap.
Simpson's paradox is a statistical phenomenon first described by E. H. Simpson in 1951 in which a trend observed in several subgroups reverses when the data are aggregated.

Judea Pearl introduced do-calculus as a formal mathematical framework that enables the computation of interventional probabilities P(Y|do(X)) from observational data when the underlying causal graph is known.
The IonQ Aria quantum computer is a trapped‑ion quantum processor that can be accessed programmatically through the Qiskit IonQ provider.
Qiskit version 1.4.3 includes the Aer module, which provides a high‑performance classical simulator for executing and testing quantum circuits.
NumPy is a Python library that supplies array programming capabilities and is widely used for scientific computing and numerical analysis.
SciPy is a Python library that offers fundamental algorithms for scientific computing, including optimization, integration, and linear algebra routines.
Matplotlib is a Python library that provides a 2D graphics environment for creating static, animated, and interactive visualizations.
The National Research Foundation of Korea funds research projects, including the grant numbered 2020R1F1A1067619, which supports scientific investigations in Korea.

The authors propose EXCAP, an explainable causal aggregation patterns framework that integrates an attention-based segmenter, a causally disentangled decoder guided by a pre-trained causal graph, and a latent aggregation loss to enforce separability and stability in the representation space.

The authors prove that the EXCAP explanation mapping is globally Lipschitz continuous with constant equal to the product of the Lipschitz constants of its constituent operators, guaranteeing that the norm of explanation changes is bounded by the norm of input perturbations.

In classification experiments on the Epilepsy dataset, the authors report that masking the top 15% most attributed inputs according to EXCAP reduces AUROC by 63.53% and AUPRC by 40.07%, which is a larger degradation than any baseline method.

The authors demonstrate that removing the wavelet-based trend extractor from EXCAP reduces AUROC on the Wafer dataset from 0.9948 to 0.9827, a statistically significant drop exceeding one standard deviation.

The authors show that the computational complexity of EXCAP scales linearly with the product of the number of variables and sequence length (O(NT)), and empirically achieve an average inference time of 42.3 ms per batch of 64 samples on an RTX 3090 GPU.

The authors report that the latent aggregation loss in EXCAP enforces a margin δ between high‑attention and low‑attention segment embeddings, and that training without this loss results in a post‑masking AUROC stability metric of 0.0140, significantly worse than the complete model’s stability of 0.0120 on the Epilepsy dataset.

The authors evaluate EXCAP on the ETT long‑horizon forecasting benchmark and achieve a mean squared error of 0.2475, which is lower than the MSE of 0.2765 obtained when the causal disentanglement component is removed.

The authors state that the pre‑trained causal graph used in EXCAP is static and does not adapt during training, which they identify as a limitation that may hinder modeling of dynamically evolving causal dependencies.
The transformer architecture replaced recurrence with self‑attention, enabling parallel computation and global‑context integration compared with recurrent neural networks (Vaswani et al., 2017).

Large language models such as GPT‑3, PaLM, and LLaMA employ billions of parameters, mixture‑of‑experts routing, and optimized data pipelines to achieve strong few‑shot generalization and emergent reasoning (Brown et al., 2020; Chowdhery et al., 2022; Touvron et al., 2023).

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback and instruction tuning enable goal‑directed behavior and safer interaction in large language models such as GPT‑4 (Lambert 2025; OpenAI GPT‑4 Technical Report, 2023).

The ReAct framework implements a reason‑act‑reflect loop by interleaving reasoning traces with tool calls, thereby transforming a static large language model into an adaptive interactive agent (Yao et al., 2023).

The AutoGen framework enables multi‑agent coordination by assigning specialized roles (Planner, Research, Writer, Reviewer) and facilitating iterative communication for research‑task execution (Wu et al., 2023).

Agentic AI systems increase computational requirements beyond standard large language model inference because they involve long interaction loops, frequent tool calls, and continuous context expansion (Section 6.5 of the paper).
Arabic language model evaluation benchmarks report that leading models achieve only about 30 % accuracy on culturally grounded reasoning tasks (Sibaee et al., 2025).

Empirical studies comparing large language model performance with human experts on complex programming problems show that the models score significantly lower than humans in multi‑step problem‑solving challenges (Koubaa et al., 2023).

The authors state that agentic AI systems operate autonomously, independently planning and making decisions to achieve complex goals, distinguishing them from traditional AI agents that perform specific tasks based on set instructions [45,46,79].

The study reports that agentic AI systems can reason over extended time horizons, coordinate sequences of actions, and adapt their behavior in response to changing environmental conditions, providing distinct advantages over traditional AI agents in managing dynamic and complex environments [100].

The authors identify that bias in agentic AI systems can originate from skewed data collection and labeling at the perception stage, which may encode existing social inequalities and be amplified by reasoning processes in domains such as healthcare, criminal justice, and recruitment [BAD discussion].

The paper highlights that accountability for adverse outcomes of autonomous agentic AI systems becomes distributed across developers, deployers, and the autonomous agents themselves, creating a moral crumple zone where responsibility attribution is challenging [78].

The authors report that the execution module of agentic AI must respect safety boundaries, enable human oversight or intervention when required, and ensure traceability of actions performed, to mitigate risks of physically harmful autonomous decisions [execution discussion].

The study demonstrates that in resource‑constrained networking, agentic AI agents using multi‑agent reinforcement learning can balance latency, energy consumption, and information freshness (AoI) objectives, achieving improved performance compared to fixed scheduling approaches [Table 4, references 63‑71].

The authors describe that orbital edge computing (OEC) leverages onboard processing to pre‑process and compress satellite data, reducing downlink bandwidth requirements and enabling more complex tasks to be performed in space [23].

The paper notes that integrating large‑language‑model‑based reasoning with tool‑use (e.g., external knowledge bases) can reduce hallucination rates in agentic AI by grounding model outputs in verifiable information, improving factual accuracy in high‑stakes domains such as healthcare and autonomous control [60].

The authors curated a GenAI Value Safety Incident Repository (GVSIR) containing 1,126 real-world incidents, derived from multi-source data collection including news outlets, AI incident databases, and academic literature.

The authors released the GenAI Value Safety Scale (GVS-Scale) and the accompanying benchmark (GVS-Bench) on GitHub at https://github.com/acl2026/GVS-Bench, providing 266 test cases across text, image, video, and audio modalities.
The authors employed GPT-5.2 as an LLM-as-a-judge to score model responses on a 0–10 scale for value safety, with each test case prompting three independent generations per model.
Human experts reviewed a random 40% sample (648 of 1,620) of LLM-assigned scores, and only 17 scores were revised, indicating high consistency between LLM and human judgments.
The European Union’s Artificial Intelligence Act classifies AI systems into risk categories and mandates a rights‑based, risk‑based regulatory framework, as cited by the authors.

The NIST AI Risk Management Framework identifies core principles of transparency, accountability, fairness, human‑rights protection, and technical robustness for trustworthy AI, as referenced by the authors.

UNESCO’s 2021 Recommendation on the Ethics of Artificial Intelligence includes principles such as human dignity, fairness, transparency, accountability, and human oversight, as mentioned in the paper.
The OECD’s 2019 AI Principles, adopted by the United Nations, emphasize inclusive growth, sustainable development, and respect for human rights, as cited in the document.
The study evaluated the multi‑agent evaluation loop on 900 adversarial prompts from MedSafetyBench, balanced across nine AMA principle categories with 100 queries per category.
DeepSeek R1 converged on 94.2% of prompts within a maximum of five iterations, requiring a mean of 2.34 iterations per prompt.
Med‑PaLM converged on 91.8% of prompts within a maximum of five iterations, requiring a mean of 2.67 iterations per prompt.
The multi‑agent evaluation loop reduced AMA ethics violation scores by 89.1% for DeepSeek R1 and by 85.4% for Med‑PaLM.
The multi‑agent evaluation loop achieved risk‑downgrade rates of 92.1% for DeepSeek R1 and 90.6% for Med‑PaLM, moving responses from Safety Risk Assessment Level 3 or higher to Level 1 or 2.
The system used two evaluator agents, LLaMA 3.1 for AMA ethics assessment and Phi‑4 for Safety Risk Assessment (SRA‑5) scoring.
The evaluation loop operated with inference settings of temperature 0.7, top‑p 0.9, maximum tokens 512, and a maximum of five iterations per prompt.
The pipeline processed approximately 150 queries per hour on four A100 GPUs, with a mean latency of 8.5 seconds per converged query.

The authors used the publicly available MIMIC‑IV dataset (version 3.1) to construct a Neo4j knowledge graph containing 5,973 nodes and 5,963 relationships representing the clinical data of 10 patients.
The authors evaluated the MediGRAF system on a set of 141 curated question‑answer pairs derived from MIMIC‑IV data, covering simple, medium, and complex query categories.

For simple queries (n = 100), the authors reported that the hybrid MediGRAF approach achieved 100 % accuracy and 100 % recall, whereas a Cypher‑only baseline achieved 80 % accuracy with a recall of 0.8.

For medium‑complexity queries (n = 31), the authors reported that the hybrid MediGRAF approach achieved 100 % accuracy and 100 % recall, while the Cypher‑only baseline achieved 51.6 % accuracy, precision of 0.806, recall of 0.688, and an F1‑score of 0.742.
The authors’ clinical expert evaluation of 10 complex queries yielded mean accuracy scores of 4.40 ± 1.02 and 4.90 ± 0.30 on a 5‑point Likert scale, with zero safety violations recorded.
The authors generated 1536‑dimensional vector embeddings for 89 free‑text clinical documents (25 discharge summaries and 64 radiology reports) using OpenAI’s text‑embedding‑3‑small model.
The authors implemented the Text2Cypher component of MediGRAF with GPT‑4o‑mini to automatically translate natural‑language clinical questions into Cypher queries for Neo4j.
The authors cited Stothers and Nguyen (2020) to support the claim that Neo4j can outperform PostgreSQL for healthcare graph queries involving complex traversals.
The authors manually screened approximately 1,800 publications from 2019 to 2025 using predefined inclusion/exclusion criteria and retained 74 AI-for-HPC papers for analysis.
The authors grouped the 74 AI-for-HPC papers into six application areas, finding that scheduling accounts for 30% of the papers, making it the most active research area.
The authors report that graph neural networks and time‑series models improve anomaly detection in production HPC telemetry by capturing spatio‑temporal dependencies.
The authors state that domain‑specialized language models for HPC outperform general‑purpose large language models on targeted coding and automation tasks.
The Green‑TOP500 dataset indicates that the average energy efficiency (PFLOPS/Watt) of the top‑500 HPC systems rose from 0.6 in June 2014 to 15.2 in June 2024.
The EAR (Energy‑Aware Runtime) plugin integrated with SLURM reduces power consumption by applying a supervised linear‑regression model to predict job energy usage.
The Alpa framework achieves a 3.5× speedup on two nodes and a 9.7× speedup on four nodes for Wide‑ResNet training, reaching 80 % linear scaling efficiency on four nodes.
The DeepSeek‑Coder large language model provides a 2× inference speedup compared with larger LLMs when used for code generation in HPC contexts.

The authors propose SPGCL, a graph contrastive learning framework that integrates a lightweight stochastic edge removal stage with a truncated singular value decomposition (SVD)-guided edge recovery stage to generate diverse yet structure‑aware graph views.

The authors report that SPGCL achieves 83.10% node classification accuracy on the Cora benchmark dataset, surpassing the 80.10% accuracy of the baseline Graph Convolutional Network (GCN) under the same experimental settings.
The authors claim that the SVD‑guided scoring mechanism can recover accidentally removed important edges with high probability, thereby mitigating the adverse effects of random edge deletion.

The authors demonstrate that removing the global consistency loss L from the training objective reduces classification accuracy on Cora from 83.10% to 82.20%, indicating that the loss contributes approximately 0.9% performance gain.

The authors show that the two‑stage perturbation design outperforms naive variants; specifically, on the Cora dataset the “SVD & Edge” variant (applying SVD before edge dropping) attains 68.10% accuracy, whereas SPGCL attains 83.10% accuracy.

The authors find that under a structural noise scenario where 30% of edges are randomly removed, SPGCL’s accuracy on Cora declines by only 6.4% (from 83.10% to 76.6%), while GCN’s accuracy drops by 9.7% (from 80.10% to 70.4%).
The authors implement the truncated SVD computation in SPGCL using the randomized SVD algorithm described in Halko, Martinsson, and Tropp (2011) [25].

The authors incorporate a contrastive fusion module that aligns node embeddings across the original and perturbed views by applying an InfoNCE loss at each GNN layer and a global similarity‑matrix consistency loss based on the Frobenius norm of the difference between view‑wise similarity matrices.

The GT-Score is defined as GT = μ·ln(z)·r² / (σ·d), where μ is the mean strategy return per observation, μ_benchmark is the mean buy‑and‑hold return per observation, σ is the standard deviation of strategy returns, N is the number of return observations, z = (μ − μ_benchmark) / (σ/√N), r² is the R‑squared measuring consistency of returns, and d is the downside deviation.
The study used historical daily OHLCV data for the top 50 S&P 500 companies by market capitalization from January 2010 through December 2024, sourced via the yFinance API.

The study performed walk‑forward validation with a 4‑year training window, a 2‑year validation window, a 1‑year step size, and a 30‑day embargo, generating nine sequential train/validation splits per asset covering validation periods 2014–2024.
In walk‑forward validation, the GT-Score achieved a generalization ratio of 0.365, which represents a 98 % improvement over baseline objective functions whose average generalization ratio was 0.185.

In a Monte Carlo analysis comprising 9,000 optimization trials (15 random seeds, three technical trading strategies, and four loss functions), the GT-Score produced a mean out‑of‑sample test return of 43.6 % and a generalization ratio of 0.183, which is 56 % higher than the 0.117 ratio obtained by the baseline loss functions.

Paired t‑tests on Monte Carlo out‑of‑sample returns indicated that the GT-Score had statistically significantly lower mean returns than the Sharpe‑Ratio loss (p = 0.014, Cohen’s d = ‑0.041), the Sortino‑Ratio loss (p < 0.001, Cohen’s d = ‑0.083), and the Simple loss (p < 0.001, Cohen’s d = ‑0.086).

The GT-Score‑selected strategies executed an average of 32.4 trades per test window, compared with approximately 21.0 trades for baseline strategies, and transaction‑cost sensitivity analysis showed that the relative ranking of objective functions remained unchanged for per‑side costs up to 10 basis points.

The GT-Score is less appropriate when the number of trades is low (fewer than 50 trades), when strategies exhibit highly skewed return distributions, or when real‑time adaptation is required because the statistical components add computational overhead.

The Delay-Aware Markov Decision Process (DA-MDP) formalism transforms a multi-step delayed MDP into a standard MDP with an augmented state, enabling the use of traditional reinforcement learning algorithms (see Chen et al., 2021 [25]).

State augmentation with past states and actions recovers approximate Markovianity in delayed environments but increases input dimensionality linearly with the delay horizon (see Nath et al., 2021 [24] and Chen et al., 2021 [25]).

Deep Recurrent Q-Networks (DRQN) replace part of a feedforward Q-network with an LSTM to handle missing or flickering observations in partially observable Markov decision processes (see Hausknecht & Stone, 2015 [10]).
Domain randomization, originally introduced for robotics, randomizes environment parameters during training to improve simulation‑to‑real‑world transfer of policies (see Miranda et al., 2023 [47]).
Control Barrier Functions enforce forward invariance of safe sets via real‑time quadratic‑program safety filters (see Ames et al., 2019 [64]).
Random delays in networked control systems can destabilize controllers designed under ideal latency assumptions (see Zhang et al., 2001 [19] and Hespanha et al., 2007 [16]).

Model‑based reinforcement learning methods that learn dynamics models can compensate for observation delays by forecasting the current state, improving performance in continuous‑control benchmarks (see Karamzade et al., 2024 [38]).

Safe reinforcement learning methods based on Constrained Markov Decision Processes incorporate safety constraints into the optimization objective, providing formal safety guarantees (see Kushwaha et al., 2015 [59] and Achiam et al., 2017 [60]).

The authors propose Fed-Listing, a passive gradient-based label distribution inference attack for horizontal federated graph neural networks that leverages only final-layer gradients exchanged during training.

The authors evaluate Fed-Listing on four benchmark graph datasets (Cora, Citeseer, PubMed, Amazon Computers) using three GNN architectures (GCN, GraphSAGE, GIN) and report that Fed-Listing achieves lower Manhattan distance and Jensen‑Shannon divergence and higher cosine similarity than random guessing, Decaf, and EC‑LDA baselines.

The authors demonstrate that under the equal‑proportion client partitioning scenario, Fed-Listing attains a Jensen‑Shannon divergence of 0.000 on the Cora dataset with the GCN architecture, outperforming Decaf (0.001) and EC‑LDA (0.003).

The authors show that applying differential‑privacy noise with ε=2 reduces the attack effectiveness of Fed-Listing on the Citeseer dataset, increasing Jensen‑Shannon divergence from 0.000 to 0.003 while preserving model accuracy.

The authors find that increasing the number of shadow federated learning processes improves Fed-Listing’s cosine similarity, achieving a similarity of 0.99 on the Amazon Computers dataset when 14 shadow processes are used.

The authors report that the auxiliary dataset used for shadow training consists of 20 % of the original data and that using this proportion yields the best attack performance across all partition scenarios.
The authors implement Fed-Listing using the Flower federated learning framework (v1.19.0) and PyTorch (v2.7.1) with PyTorch Geometric (v2.6.1).
The authors state that the label distribution inference attack problem in federated graph neural networks has not been previously explored, making Fed-Listing the first method addressing this threat.
The authors implemented a RoBERTa‑Base model for English hope speech detection on the PolyHope‑M dataset and reported a weighted F1‑score of 0.818 and an accuracy of 81.8 % on the test split.
The authors implemented an XLM‑RoBERTa‑Base model for German hope speech detection on the PolyHope‑M dataset and reported a weighted F1‑score of 0.786 and an accuracy of 78.5 % on the test split.
The authors’ English system achieved a macro‑averaged F1‑score of 0.818 and was ranked 14th out of 16 participating teams in the PolyHope‑M shared task.
The authors’ German system achieved a macro‑averaged F1‑score of 0.786 and was ranked 12th out of 12 participating teams in the PolyHope‑M shared task.

The authors applied a preprocessing pipeline that converted all characters to lowercase, removed URLs, punctuation, symbols, emojis, and retained German umlauts (ä, ö, ü, ß) and whitespace before tokenization.

The authors used the PolyHope‑M dataset, which contains 4,541 English training instances (2,296 hope, 2,245 not‑hope) and 5,262 English test instances, as well as 11,573 German training instances and 5,262 German test instances.
The authors trained the English RoBERTa model with a learning rate of 1 × 10⁻⁵, batch size of 16, dropout rate of 0.1, maximum sequence length of 514, and the AdamW optimizer.
The authors cited prior work indicating that transformer‑based models improve F1‑score by 9–12 % over traditional machine‑learning approaches for hope speech detection.
The authors propose FUSE-Flow, a frame-wise, stateless, weighted point cloud fusion framework that combines measurement confidence and 3D distance consistency to fuse multi-view depth observations.

The authors implement an adaptive spatial hashing-based weighted aggregation method that partitions 3D space according to local point cloud density and selects representative points per cell for fusion.

The authors report that FUSE-Flow achieves an average reconstruction error (MC error) of 9.2 mm and a frame rate of 62 FPS on Platform 1 (Intel i7‑9750H CPU, RTX 2060 GPU, 32 GB RAM) when using a single RGB‑D camera.

The authors report that FUSE-Flow achieves an average reconstruction error of 12.9 mm and a frame rate of 56 FPS on Platform 2 (Intel i7‑12700 CPU, RTX 3060 GPU, 64 GB RAM) when using four RGB‑D cameras.
The authors claim that FUSE-Flow’s computational cost grows approximately linearly with the number of cameras, unlike voxel‑based fusion methods whose cost grows superlinearly.

The authors compare FUSE-Flow to PatchmatchNet and R3D3 and state that FUSE-Flow yields lower MC error (e.g., 9.2 mm vs. 11.8 mm for PatchmatchNet in static multi‑camera scenes) while maintaining higher FPS.

The authors define measurement confidence for each depth pixel as a function of depth gradient magnitude and local depth variance, with a confidence threshold set to 0.6 to suppress low‑confidence points.

The authors evaluate FUSE-Flow on three hardware platforms (Platform 1 laptop, Platform 2 desktop, Platform 3 server) and demonstrate real‑time performance (≥31 FPS) even on the low‑end laptop configuration.
The study reports that Explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) achieves 42.9 ± 0.2 % accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark using an average of 25.0 tokens per answer.
The study reports that Latent Chain-of-Thought (Latent CoT, also called Coconut) achieves 34.1 ± 1.5 % accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark using an average of 8.2 tokens per answer.
The study reports that Explicit Chain-of-Thought (CoT) achieves 77.5 ± 1.9 % accuracy on the ProsQA benchmark using an average of 49.4 tokens per answer.
The study reports that Latent Chain-of-Thought (Latent CoT) achieves 97.0 ± 0.3 % accuracy on the ProsQA benchmark using an average of 14.2 tokens per answer.

The study reports that removing curriculum learning from the Coconut training procedure reduces Latent CoT accuracy on the ProntoQA dataset from 99.8 % to 52.4 % (average tokens 9.0 in both conditions).
The study introduces the Symbolic Index I, defined as the maximum token probability at a reasoning step, which ranges between 1/|V| and 1.

The study proves (Theorem 4.12) that the KL divergence between the uniform exploration prior q and the model’s output distribution p satisfies D(q‖p) ≥ −log B − log I + (B−1) log (1−I), where B is the branching factor and I is the Symbolic Index.

The study proves (Theorem 5.1) that training Latent CoT without curriculum learning leads to a permanent performance gap because the maximum‑likelihood estimator converges to a biased policy with success rate bounded away from the expert optimum.
The authors formulate self‑play finetuning as a min–max game between a language‑model policy and a regularized implicit reward player that is parameterized by the model itself.
The authors prove that using a mixed Pearson χ² divergence regularizer yields a bounded optimal reward that lies within the interval [−1/c, 1/c].
The authors show that the duality gap of their self‑play imitation algorithm is upper‑bounded by O((D + B)R²/√K), where D, B, R and K are defined in the game‑theoretic analysis.

The authors' χ²‑based self‑play imitation finetuning algorithm (SPIF) achieves higher accuracy than the SPIN baseline on the Arc‑Challenge benchmark for the Qwen‑3‑4B model after three self‑play iterations (55.12 % for SPIN vs 57.11 % for SPIF).

The authors' χ²‑based self‑play imitation finetuning algorithm (SPIF) achieves higher accuracy than the SPIN baseline on the MMLU benchmark for the Mistral‑7B model after three self‑play iterations (68.06 % for SPIN vs 68.83 % for SPIF).
The authors observe that the reward magnitude produced by SPIF is significantly smaller than that produced by SPIN, which results in more stable gradient‑norm dynamics during training.
The authors demonstrate that linear SPIN with an identity link function minimizes the total‑variation distance between the model policy and the expert policy.
The authors demonstrate that non‑linear SPIN with a logistic link function minimizes the KL divergence between the model policy and the expert policy.

The authors designed a triphibious robot named TriphiBot that combines a quadcopter with two passive wheels and uses an eccentric center of gravity to align thrust with motion, achieving efficient ground motion without extra actuators.
The authors implemented a unified propulsion system based on field‑oriented control (FOC) that provides fast and precise bidirectional thrust control across air and water.
The authors reported that the propulsion unit achieves up to 95.37 % energy savings for ground motion compared to aerial flight.
The authors measured an underwater specific thrust of 0.825 N/W for the propulsion unit, which is 14 times greater than the specific thrust of a traditional ESC (0.059 N/W).
The authors demonstrated aerial trajectory tracking of an 8‑shaped path with a root‑mean‑square error of 0.096 m.
The authors achieved a maximum underwater speed of 2 m/s and acceleration of 2 m/s² while tracking an 8‑shaped trajectory.
The authors showed that the robot can transition from water to air by lowering rotor speed to dive and then increasing speed to lift off, completing the transition in approximately 1.5 seconds.
The authors observed that the robot’s ground clearance reduces from 11 cm to 2 cm when transitioning from flight to terrestrial mode due to a 90° rotation around the y‑axis.

The authors introduce PENCIL, an encoder‑only plain Transformer that operates on fixed‑budget sampled local subgraphs for link prediction, and achieve state‑of‑the‑art mean reciprocal rank (MRR) of 66.56 ± 0.19 on the OGBL‑PPA benchmark while using 22 × fewer learnable parameters than the next best ID‑based method.

The authors demonstrate that adding a multiplicative residual connection to PENCIL improves performance, yielding a mean reciprocal rank (MRR) increase of +7.59 on the Cora dataset compared with a version of PENCIL without the residual.

The authors show that PENCIL can estimate classic pairwise structural heuristics—including Common Neighbors, Adamic–Adar, Resource Allocation, Katz index, shortest‑path distance, and PageRank—without explicit handcrafted features, achieving root‑mean‑square error (RMSE) comparable to or lower than graph neural network baselines on the Citeseer dataset.
The authors prove that PENCIL’s link‑prediction function is permutation‑invariant in distribution under endpoint‑constrained random node relabelings, as formalized in Theorem 4.1.

The authors report that PENCIL converges after only 0.5 training epochs on the OGBL‑Citation2 dataset, 8 epochs on OGBL‑DDI, and 15 epochs on OGBL‑PPA, which is substantially fewer than the dozens to hundreds of epochs required by pure GNN models on the same benchmarks.

The authors find that orthogonal initialization of PENCIL’s input‑projection matrix leads to the fastest and most stable convergence and the highest final MRR across Cora, Citeseer, and PubMed, whereas low‑rank and mean‑shifted Gaussian initializations result in slower convergence and lower MRR.

The authors measure that PENCIL with three Transformer layers requires 10.2 million parameters and a training time per mini‑batch of 0.019 ± 0.045 seconds on the OGBL‑Collab dataset, which is comparable to Graph Attention Network (GAT) with three layers that uses 1.32 million parameters and 0.021 ± 0.078 seconds per batch.

The authors demonstrate that PENCIL’s batching strategy based on padding and stacking isolated subgraphs reduces batch‑construction overhead and peak RAM usage compared with PyG’s from‑data‑list collation, as shown by lower batching time and memory consumption on the OGBL‑Collab dataset.
The authors report that AXE achieves a zero-shot F1 score of 88.10% on the SWDE benchmark, surpassing the best supervised baseline WebLM which scores 87.57%.
The authors report that the pruning adaptor reduces the average token context length on the SWDE dataset from 16,581 tokens to 350.6 tokens, representing a 97.9% reduction.
The authors report that removing the Grounded XPath Resolution (GXR) exact algorithm decreases AXE's SWDE F1 score from 88.37% to 83.95%, a drop of 4.42%.

The authors report that AXE consists of three main modules: a preprocessor that cleans and chunks HTML, an AI extractor that performs pruning and query answering, and a postprocessor that grounds outputs to source nodes.
The authors report that they trained three LoRA adaptors (pruning, QA, schema) on the Qwen-0.6B backbone with rank 64, learning rate 1.7×10⁻⁵, and two epochs of training.
The authors report that AXE attains an F1 score of 88.10% on the WebSRC dataset in a zero-shot setting, outperforming the WebLM large model which achieves 87.57%.
The authors report that an ablation removing the pruning adaptor reduces AXE's SWDE F1 from 88.37% to 87.71%, a decrease of 0.66%.
The authors report that increasing the HTML chunk size improves extraction performance up to 3000–4000 tokens, after which performance plateaus, as shown in Figure 5.

The authors implemented the Vibe Coding pedagogical framework, which sanctions LLM-assisted code generation, requires mandatory prompt logging, and uses reflection-based assessment, in a senior-level undergraduate NLP course during the Fall 2025 semester at King Saud University.

The study enrolled 19 senior undergraduate students in the NLP course and collected quantitative feedback using Likert‑scale items (1–5) on engagement, conceptual learning, and assessment fairness, yielding mean scores between 4.4 and 4.6.
The mandatory prompt logging component received a mean usefulness rating of 3.79 (SD = 1.13) on a 5‑point Likert scale, indicating moderate perceived value among the 19 students.
The grading shift from code quality to conceptual reflection was rated as appropriate with a mean of 4.26 (SD = 0.99) on a 5‑point Likert scale by the 19 students.
The use of LLMs in lab activities was rated as engaging with a mean of 4.42 (SD = 0.84) on a 5‑point Likert scale by the 19 students.

Students reported that the Vibe Coding approach reduced cognitive load and allowed more time for conceptual understanding, as evidenced by qualitative comments such as “It made coding feel comfortable and with low pressure.”

The final project, involving translation and modeling of the Arabic PIQA dataset, was rated positively with a mean overall experience rating of 4.37 (SD = 0.83) on a 5‑point Likert scale by the 19 students.
The authors identified time pressure as a persistent challenge, with the “time allocation sufficient” item receiving a mean rating of 3.53 (SD = 1.43) on a 5‑point Likert scale.

The authors constructed two‑dimensional slices of the potential energy surface by varying atomic positions along selected Wyckoff degrees of freedom while keeping the crystal symmetry fixed, enabling direct visual comparison of interatomic potentials with DFT‑calculated surfaces.

The authors evaluated nine pretrained universal interatomic potential models—MACE_medium, MACE_OB2, MACE_OB3, MACE_MPA-0, MACE_OMAT-0, MACE_MATPES-PBE, SevenNet0, ORB v2, and CHGNet—using the same crystal structures and Wyckoff‑degree perturbations.

The authors found that most evaluated models accurately capture local minima and reproduce the general curvature of the potential energy surface near equilibrium configurations, but exhibit significant discrepancies and artifacts in regions far from equilibrium or when atomic arrangements become unphysical.

The authors observed that the MACE_MPA-0 model predicts a distinct spurious local minimum in a region where DFT calculations and other MACE models indicate no stable configuration, resulting in an energy range difference of approximately 8 eV per atom between the highest and lowest predicted energies on the corresponding two‑dimensional energy slice.

The authors reported that the CHGNet model and the SevenNet0 model display noisy behavior and an unphysical drop in predicted energy in regions where atomic overlap becomes significant, as revealed by the symmetry‑constrained two‑dimensional energy landscapes.

The authors demonstrated that the ORB v2 model predicts an energy drop in regions of close atomic proximity, but the overall energy range on the examined slice is narrower, with a difference of about 8 eV per atom between the highest and lowest energies, which is smaller than the range observed for most other models.

The authors showed that the MACE_MATPES-PBE model consistently identifies a different local minimum compared with all other models for two distinct Wyckoff perturbations, and that this alternative minimum persists after lifting symmetry constraints and performing a full geometry relaxation.

The authors implemented a distance‑cost function that defines a minimum allowed interatomic distance as the sum of the Wigner‑Seitz radii of the two atoms; configurations violating this threshold are excluded from the generated two‑dimensional potential energy surfaces to avoid unphysical atomic proximities.

The authors trained OPENSEAL, a 7‑billion‑parameter Southeast Asian LLM, by continual pretraining on 34.7 billion tokens of parallel data for 180 hours on eight NVIDIA H200 GPUs, incurring a training cost of under US $12,000.

The authors found that the Parallel‑Only continual pretraining strategy yields higher BLEU scores for translation from Southeast Asian languages to English than the Multilingual strategy when both are trained on a 1‑billion‑parameter model with the same token budget.

The authors reported that the Parallel‑Only 7‑billion‑parameter model achieved a BLEU score of 31.12 for English→Indonesian translation, surpassing the Multilingual 7‑billion‑parameter model’s score of 22.03 under identical training conditions.

The authors demonstrated that the Parallel‑Only 7‑billion‑parameter model attained an XNLI average accuracy of 70.20, which is statistically indistinguishable from the Sailor 2 8‑billion‑parameter model’s 74.50 (p < 0.01) and exceeds the SeaLLM v3 7‑billion‑parameter model’s 70.53.

The authors showed that replacing parallel data blocks with multilingual SEA‑PILE‑v2 data (Multilingual‑Replacement) reduces translation BLEU scores from 31.12 to 22.03 for English→Indonesian and from 38.04 to 23.19 for Indonesian→English on the 7‑billion‑parameter model.

The authors observed that layer‑20 of the Parallel‑Only model exhibits weaker English‑language probability than the Multilingual model, while layers‑24 and‑30 show a gradual shift toward target‑language probabilities during training.

The authors reported that training the 1‑billion‑parameter model on 10 billion tokens required approximately 11 hours, whereas training the 7‑billion‑parameter model on 34.7 billion tokens required about 180 hours on the same hardware.
The authors released OPENSEAL as a fully open‑source model with publicly available weights, training data, and code, making it the first open‑weight Southeast Asian LLM.

The authors report that the Superman model achieves a Normalized Mean Per Joint Position Error (N‑MPJPE) of 39.41 mm on the Human3.6M dataset for 3D pose estimation, which is lower than all baseline methods listed in Table 2.

The authors report that the Superman model achieves a Mean Per Joint Position Error (MPJPE) of 51.61 mm on the Human3.6M dataset for 3D pose estimation, outperforming every baseline method in the same table.

The authors report that, on the motion prediction (MP) task with an 80 ms prediction horizon on Human3.6M, the Superman model with Motion‑Aware Fine‑Tuning (MAFT) attains an N‑MPJPE of 26.13 mm, surpassing MotionBERT (29.94 mm) and all other listed baselines.

The authors report that, on the motion in‑betweening (MIB) task with a 320 ms horizon on Human3.6M, the Superman model attains an N‑MPJPE of 30.61 mm, which is lower than MotionBERT (42.37 mm) and all other compared methods.

The authors report that the Vision‑Guided Motion Tokenizer (VGMT) employs a hybrid codebook containing 8 192 codes of dimension 2 048, and that 65.4 % of the codes remain active during inference on Human3.6M.

The authors report that, when evaluated zero‑shot on the unseen 3DPW dataset, the Superman model achieves an MPJPE of 44.90 mm for motion prediction, outperforming Skeleton‑in‑Context (54.62 mm) and MotionGPT3 (47.66 mm).
The authors report that the Motion‑Aware Fine‑Tuning (MAFT) module adds less than 0.2 % trainable parameters to the overall model architecture.

The authors report that the unified model is built on the Qwen2.5‑VL‑7B large language model with LoRA rank 8, resulting in 1 235 M trainable parameters, which constitute 12.87 % of the total 9 605 M parameters.
The authors employ a categorical hidden Markov model (HMM) to predict client selection conflict probability from sparse historical selection sequences in multi‑server federated learning.
The authors integrate a fairness‑aware reward term r = −L − C + α·f into the reinforcement‑learning objective to promote balanced client participation while minimizing latency and conflict costs.
The authors evaluate the RL‑CRP framework on the CIFAR‑10 dataset with both IID and non‑IID data partitions and report test accuracies of 67.68 % (IID) and 61.32 % (non‑IID) after convergence.
The authors compare RL‑CRP with the baseline ENSAC algorithm in a two‑server scenario and observe that RL‑CRP achieves significantly faster reward convergence and a higher final reward than ENSAC.

The authors demonstrate that increasing the number of servers from two to four leads to a monotonic increase in client‑selection conflict count for all evaluated algorithms, but RL‑CRP consistently yields fewer conflicts than ENSAC and FedAvg.

The authors show that removing the fairness component from RL‑CRP (RL‑CRP w/o fairness) results in lower test accuracy and a reduced number of conflicts compared to the full RL‑CRP, indicating a trade‑off between fairness and conflict minimization.
The authors set the fairness weight α to 100, the maximum latency L to 40 seconds, and the communication bandwidth to 100 MHz in their experimental configuration for multi‑server federated learning.
The authors state that the k‑colorability problem is NP‑complete for all integers k ≥ 3, referencing Garey and Johnson (1976).
The authors claim that the Goldreich–Micali–Wigderson (GMW) zero‑knowledge identification protocol can be generalized from 3‑colorability to arbitrary k‑colorability for any k ≥ 3.

The authors report that applying the Fiat–Shamir transform to the generalized GMW protocol yields a non‑interactive signature scheme whose asymptotic signature size is reduced from O(t n) to O(t log n) by incorporating Merkle‑tree vector commitments.

The authors present experimental evidence that for generated k‑partite graphs with at least 60 vertices (n ≥ 60) and the chosen density parameters, neither the classical DSatur heuristic nor the custom graph neural network attacker recovers the planted secret coloring.
The authors claim that the chromatic number of Erdős–Rényi random graphs G(n, ½) observed in their experiments follows the empirical power‑law χ(n, ½) ≈ 1.13 · n^{0.54}.

The authors assert that the security proof (Proposition 4.1) shows that the Fiat‑Shamir signature with Merkle compression is existentially unforgeable under adaptive chosen‑message attack (EUF‑CMA) in the random‑oracle model, assuming (i) the commitment scheme is statistically hiding and computationally binding, (ii) the hash function is collision‑resistant, and (iii) recovering a valid k‑coloring of the public graph is computationally hard.

The authors specify that the prototype implementation uses SHA‑256 for both the hash function H and the commitment hash, and that for parameters n = 200, t = 256, the resulting signature size without path sharing is approximately 144.5 KiB, matching the analytical formula.

The authors note that the Merkle tree authentication path length is ⌈log₂ n⌉ sibling hashes, each of λ = 256 bits, leading to a per‑round overhead of O(λ log n) bits, consistent with Merkle’s original construction (1987).
Robotic deep anterior lamellar keratoplasty (DALK) reduces graft rejection risk compared to penetrating keratoplasty.
Intraoperative M-mode optical coherence tomography provides high temporal resolution depth traces at a fixed lateral position.
Topology-aware loss formulations improve structural coherence in medical image segmentation by enforcing global anatomical constraints.
The authors' topology-aware UNeXt segmentation pipeline achieved an end-to-end throughput exceeding 80 Hz on a single GPU during M-mode OCT processing.
The authors' topology-aware UNeXt segmentation pipeline achieved a mean absolute epithelium boundary error of 0.31 pixels (0.9796 µm) on the hybrid rabbit-eye M-mode OCT dataset.
The authors' topology-aware UNeXt segmentation pipeline achieved a Dice coefficient of 0.9892 on the hybrid rabbit-eye M-mode OCT dataset.
The authors' topology-aware UNeXt segmentation pipeline achieved a PSNR of 36.21 dB on the hybrid rabbit-eye M-mode OCT dataset.
The prior modified U-Net method with topology-aware loss achieved a Dice coefficient of 0.9953 on the hybrid rabbit-eye M-mode OCT dataset.

The authors offered the new upper‑level undergraduate course "Computational Discourse and Natural Language Generation" for the first time in Fall 2025, cross‑listed between the Linguistics and Computer Science departments at the University of Texas at Austin.

The authors assembled a development team comprising three professors with expertise in discourse processing, psycholinguistics, and natural language generation, together with one postdoctoral researcher and one computational linguistics PhD student.

The authors designed three open‑ended assignments and a final project for the course, requiring students to (i) implement decoding algorithms such as greedy, top‑p, and min‑p sampling, (ii) evaluate discourse coherence using Centering Theory and Entity Grid, and (iii) generate question‑under‑discussion (QUD) prompts from news articles.

The authors conducted an anonymous survey of the 26 students enrolled in the course (19 responded) and found that 32 % of respondents reported the course had a "good extent" of influence on their academic or career plans, while another 32 % reported influence "to a great extent".
The authors reported that, according to the course evaluation, 75 % of the students had prior knowledge of machine learning or AI and 55 % had prior knowledge of linguistics before taking the course.
The authors used the "minicons" library (Misra, 2022) in the course to provide students with flexible control over language‑model inference and evaluation.

The authors taught core discourse frameworks in the course, specifically Rhetorical Structure Theory (RST) and the Penn Discourse Treebank (PDTB), as foundational material for discourse analysis and generation.
The authors recorded a 95 % overall satisfaction rate among students in the post‑course evaluation.
The authors prove, using a theoretical lower‑bound argument, that any constant‑bandwidth BAPO‑CoT for the MAJORITY problem on inputs of length n requires Ω(n) chain‑of‑thought tokens.
The authors prove, using a theoretical lower‑bound argument, that any constant‑bandwidth BAPO‑CoT for the MATCH3_n problem on inputs of size n requires Ω(n) chain‑of‑thought tokens.
The authors prove, using a theoretical lower‑bound argument, that any constant‑bandwidth BAPO‑CoT for the REACHABILITY problem on directed graphs with m edges requires Ω(m) chain‑of‑thought tokens.

The authors construct, via an explicit algorithmic design, a (0,2)‑cBAPO‑CoT that solves the MAJORITY problem in Θ(n log n) chain‑of‑thought steps, i.e., O(n log n) token complexity, for n‑bit inputs.
The authors construct, via an explicit algorithmic design, a (0,2)‑cBAPO‑CoT that solves the MATCH3_n problem in O(n) chain‑of‑thought steps, matching the Ω(n) lower bound, for inputs of size n.

The authors construct, via an explicit algorithmic design, a (0,3)‑cBAPO‑CoT that solves the REACHABILITY problem in O(n²) chain‑of‑thought steps, matching the Ω(m) lower bound (with m = Θ(n²) for dense graphs), for graphs with n nodes.

The authors conduct empirical experiments with GPT‑5.2, varying reasoning effort, and find that the number of reasoning tokens scales approximately linearly with input size for the MAJORITY, MATCH3_n, and REACHABILITY tasks, while achieving near‑perfect accuracy.

The authors conduct empirical experiments with Gemini 2.5 Pro and find that the model uses more reasoning tokens than GPT‑5.2 for the same tasks and exhibits lower accuracy on MAJORITY, with token usage exceeding the linear scaling expected from the theoretical bounds.

The authors show that the optimizer of the KL‑regularized objective J (Equation 2) exists, is unique, and has the exponential‑tilt form P̃⋆(x|y)=P(x|y)exp[α(r(x|y)+V(x,y,z))]/Z(y,z) (Equation 3) for any α>0, reward function r, and terminal values V.

The authors demonstrate that under the posterior‑identification assumption (A1), the pointwise log‑ratio α r(x|y)+V(x,y,z)−V(y,z) equals the conditional pointwise mutual information i(x;z|y) (Equation 7).

The authors prove that the reward‑value decomposition is ambiguous up to an arbitrary context‑only baseline c(y,z), because shifting r(x|y)→r(x|y)+c(y,z) and V(y,z)→V(y,z)+c(y,z) leaves the posterior P(x|y,z) unchanged (Equation 8).

The authors establish that imposing order‑independence across update directions (Assumption A2) yields the commutativity constraint r(x|y)−V(y,z)=r(z|y)−V(x,y) (Equation 13) under assumptions A1 and A2.
The authors define the conditional pointwise mutual information as i(x;z|y)=log[ P(x,z|y) / ( P(x|y) P(z|y) ) ] (Equation 1) for any discrete variables with positive probability mass.
The authors note that KL‑regularized objectives are standard in maximum‑entropy control, variational inference, and regularized reinforcement learning, citing references [6, 15, 16, 21].

The authors derive that the log‑normalizer V(y,z) equals the maximal value of the KL‑regularized objective, i.e., V(y,z)=α⁻¹ log ∑_x P(x|y) exp[α(r(x|y)+V(x,y,z))] (Equation 4), for the optimizer of (2).
The authors state that the analysis assumes three discrete random variables X, Y, Z are required to derive the algebraic results presented in Sections 4–7.
Myerson (1981) proved that the revenue‑optimal auction for a single item with independent private values is a second‑price auction with a reserve price.
Gibbard (1973) demonstrated that every onto, non‑dictatorial deterministic voting rule with at least three alternatives is manipulable (Gibbard–Satterthwaite theorem).

Dütting et al. (2017) showed that neural networks parameterizing allocation and payment functions can be trained end‑to‑end to maximize expected revenue while approximately satisfying incentive‑compatibility constraints.

Rochet (1987) established that an allocation rule is implementable in dominant‑strategy incentive‑compatible mechanisms if and only if it is cyclically monotone, and payments can be derived as subgradients of a convex potential.
The Method of Equal Shares (MES) satisfies extended justified representation (EJR) in approval‑based participatory budgeting under appropriate formulations (Peters et al., 2021).

Lee et al. (2019) introduced the Set Transformer architecture, which provides permutation‑equivariant neural networks for set‑input data, enabling anonymity and neutrality in neural social‑choice models.
An et al. (2026) constructed differentiable loss functions that approximate Condorcet‑consistent voting rules (e.g., soft Copeland), allowing gradient‑based optimization of aggregation principles.

Ahmadi et al. (2023) proved that in online strategic classification, the regret bound necessarily scales with the maximum degree Δ of the manipulation graph, establishing a fundamental limit on learnability under strategic behavior.
The authors propose the VIRAL framework, which formulates visual in-context learning as a visual‑analogy conditional generation problem and employs a frozen Diffusion Transformer (DiT) backbone.
The authors construct a large‑scale in‑context editing dataset containing 400 000 exemplar‑query quadruplets that span standard perception, image‑restoration, and open‑domain editing tasks.

The authors fine‑tune the VIRAL model using a Mixture‑of‑Experts LoRA (MoE‑LoRA) adapter with four experts (rank 64, top‑2 routing) applied to the feed‑forward network output projection, and standard LoRA (rank 64) applied to the attention query, key, and value layers.
In the interactive segmentation benchmark, the VIRAL model achieves an intersection‑over‑union (IoU) of 0.795, which exceeds the IoU of 0.348 reported for the Painter baseline.
On the deraining benchmark, the VIRAL model attains a peak‑signal‑to‑noise ratio (PSNR) of 29.67 dB, whereas the specialist CSUD model reports a PSNR of 33.31 dB.

For depth estimation, the VIRAL model records an absolute relative error (AbsRel) of 0.138 and a δ ↑ metric of 0.812, improving over the standard LoRA baseline which records AbsRel 0.143 and δ ↑ 0.803.
When evaluated on the Pascal‑5i object‑detection dataset, the VIRAL model obtains an IoU of 0.721, indicating strong out‑of‑distribution performance.
The VIRAL model demonstrates zero‑shot generalization to line‑art generation, achieving IoU 0.721, RMSE 34.82, and LPIPS 0.339 despite being trained only on Canny edge maps.
The authors report that the GraDE estimator achieves up to 114 % improvement in Spearman’s rank correlation coefficient on synthesized datasets compared with sampling‑based baselines.
The authors report that the GraDE framework discovers large‑scale frequent subgraph patterns with up to 30× higher median frequency than sampling‑based methods for subgraph size k ≥ 10.
The authors report that the GraDE estimator using the DisCo‑E model attains a Spearman’s ρ of 0.88 on NAS‑Bench‑101 for subgraph size k = 4.
The authors report that the GraDE estimator using the DisCo‑E model attains a Spearman’s ρ of 0.94 on NAS‑Bench‑101 for subgraph size k = 5.
The authors report that training the GraDE estimator on the Younger dataset for subgraph size k = 4 requires 5.97 hours of GPU time.

The authors report that the sampling phase of the GraDE framework consumes the largest proportion of total runtime as subgraph size increases, while training and beam‑search phases exhibit modest growth.
The authors report that the Younger dataset contains 14,230 unique neural architectures with a maximum of about 50,000 nodes per architecture and 314 distinct operator types.
The authors report that the Rand‑ESU sampling method draws 56,865 subgraphs to achieve a sampling density of 0.1 for subgraph size k = 4 on NAS‑Bench‑101.

The authors propose MIRROR, a fine‑tuning‑free multi‑agent framework that combines execution‑driven iterative adaptive revision (IAR) and hierarchical retrieval‑augmented generation (HRAG) to translate natural‑language optimization problems into executable Gurobi solver code.

The authors report that MIRROR achieves the highest macro‑average pass@1 accuracy of 71.88 % across five standard operations‑research benchmarks (NL4Opt, Mamo‑EasyLP, Mamo‑ComplexLP, IndustryOR, ComplexOR), surpassing all prior learning‑based and agent‑based baselines.
On the NL4Opt benchmark, the authors obtain a pass@1 accuracy of 86.50 % with MIRROR, which exceeds the next best method (COE) by 1.60 percentage points (84.90 %).

The authors construct an exemplar library L containing 602 verified optimization instances curated from OptiBench, OptMATH, and Mamo‑EasyLP, each validated against an oracle Gurobi solver for optimality before inclusion.

The authors implement HRAG as a two‑stage retrieval process: first a coarse‑grained filter using Maximal Marginal Relevance (MMR) to select diverse candidate exemplars, followed by fine‑grained reranking with a large language model to ensure semantic relevance to the target problem.

The authors describe IAR as an execution‑driven loop that activates whenever solver code execution returns a failure flag; the Modeling Revision Agent and Code Revision Agent then diagnose the error, generate structured revision tips, and iteratively refine the model and code until a correct solution is produced or a preset maximum of revision rounds is reached.

The authors evaluate MIRROR with the backbone model qwen‑plus‑2025‑09‑11 and demonstrate that hierarchical retrieval alone improves macro‑average accuracy from 67.90 % (without HRAG) to 71.88 % (full MIRROR), indicating a 4.0‑point gain attributable to HRAG.

The authors’ dual‑memory architecture consists of a local memory pool that stores per‑task agent outputs (models, code, revision tips) for immediate revision and a global memory pool that accumulates cross‑task knowledge to enable knowledge transfer across different optimization problem types.
The Economist reported in June 2025 that generative AI is turning the advertising business upside down, indicating widespread adoption of AI in the advertising industry.
The Wall Street Journal reported in May 2024 that stock photographers face job displacement due to generative AI, demonstrating AI's impact on professional photography.

Shumailov et al. (2023) identified "model collapse" as a critical degradation in model performance when a significant portion of training data consists of synthetic data, showing a risk of using AI‑generated content for model training.

Dubey et al. (2024) documented that large‑scale models such as ChatGPT and Llama experience amplified model collapse when trained on larger synthetic training sets, highlighting a feedback loop that worsens model performance.
Burns et al. (2022) found that outputs from large language models are more clustered than human‑generated text, providing empirical evidence that LLMs reduce linguistic variance.
Sourati et al. (2025) reported that the linguistic diversity of language model outputs shrinks in the age of large language models, confirming a reduction in variance of generated language.
King (2024) reported that AI‑generated music now dominates the lo‑fi streaming genre, illustrating rapid adoption of AI substitutes in creative music production.
Vaswani et al. (2017) introduced the Transformer architecture, which underlies most modern generative AI models, establishing the technical foundation for current AI capabilities.
The authors selected 1567 structures from the nmrshiftdb2 database that contain only carbon and hydrogen atoms, have up to 17 carbon atoms, and possess a one‑dimensional 13C NMR spectrum.

The authors built a conditional invertible neural network based on the i‑RevNet architecture that splits the 1024‑bit output into 128 bits representing the spectrum (Y) and 896 bits representing latent variables (Z).
The authors observed that validation loss increased after epoch 5 during training, indicating overfitting, and therefore used five epochs as the early‑stopping point for all subsequent evaluations.
The authors reported a mean F1 score of 0.1695 for spectrum prediction across ten random train/validation splits, which is substantially above the expected random‑baseline F1 of approximately 0.0643.
The authors measured maximum numerical reconstruction errors between 0.00009 and 0.00015 when inverting the network on training examples, demonstrating near‑perfect input reconstruction.
The authors found that the latent variable Z had a mean of 0.0858 and a standard deviation of 0.5861 on the validation set, deviating from the ideal standard‑normal distribution (mean 0, std 1).

The authors calculated a mean Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.1863 (range 0.0971–0.2284) between the number of 1s in real input tensors and the number of 1s in reconstructed tensors over ten runs, indicating a weak positive correlation.
The authors made the code for the invertible neural network publicly available at https://github.com/stefhk3/RevMolSpec.
The authors introduce an axiomatic framework that defines nine formal properties (axioms) for counterfactual explanations.
The authors prove impossibility theorems demonstrating that no counterfactual explainer can simultaneously satisfy certain combinations of the defined axioms.
The authors fully characterize all compatible sets of axioms and establish five one-to-one correspondences between specific subsets of axioms and families of counterfactual explainer models.
The authors present representation theorems that provide a one-to-one mapping between each identified subset of axioms and a distinct family of counterfactual explainers.

The authors analyze the computational complexity of generating counterfactual explanations and show that the decision problem DECIDEEXP(L) is linear time O(n) for explainers cSuf, sNec, and L_wf, while it is co‑NP‑complete for explainers gNec and sSuf.

The authors identify five fundamentally different types of counterfactual explanations—global necessary reasons, local necessary reasons, global sufficient reasons, local sufficient reasons, and credulous sufficient reasons.
The authors demonstrate that the axioms Coreness, Non‑Triviality, and Success cannot all be satisfied simultaneously by any counterfactual explainer.
The authors prove that an explainer satisfies both Feasibility and Sceptical Validity if and only if the explainer is a subset of the global necessary reasons family (L ⊑ sNec).

The authors introduced an autonomous ultrasonic sacral osteotomy (USO) robotic system that integrates an ultrasonic osteotome with a seven-degree-of-freedom robotic manipulator guided by an optical tracking system.

In experiments using Sawbones polyurethane foam blocks, the robotic USO (RUSO) achieved a trajectory root-mean-square error of 0.11 mm, which is an order of magnitude lower than the manual USO (MUSO) trajectory RMSE of 1.10 mm.

In the same experiments, the manual USO (MUSO) achieved a mean cut depth of 7.0 mm for a target depth of 4 mm, representing 75 % over‑penetration, whereas the robotic USO (RUSO) achieved a mean cut depth of 4.2 mm for the same target, within less than 5 % error.

For a target depth of 8 mm, the manual USO (MUSO) achieved a mean cut depth of 15.9 mm, representing approximately 100 % over‑penetration, while the robotic USO (RUSO) achieved a mean cut depth of 8.1 mm, within less than 5 % error.

The robotic USO (RUSO) completed a 100‑mm osteotomy trajectory with a target depth of 4 mm in an average active cutting time of 45 seconds, whereas the manual USO (MUSO) required an average active cutting time of 111 seconds for the same task.

The robotic USO (RUSO) produced cuts with an average executed length of 101.1 mm, closely matching the planned 100‑mm length, while manual USO (MUSO) cuts had an average executed length of 97.4 mm, with some trials as short as 96 mm.

Prior studies of computer‑assisted navigation (CAN) guided sacral osteotomies reported mean execution errors of 2–4 mm relative to the planned path and a global mean deviation of 2.52 mm across clinical cases.

The authors performed hand‑eye calibration by solving the AX = XB matrix equation using N distinct robot poses to determine the transformation between the robot base frame and the optical tracker frame.

The authors introduced TartanRGBT, an open‑source RGB‑T data‑collection platform and dataset comprising 16,943 synchronized RGB‑T image pairs captured across indoor, aerial, off‑road, and urban environments, with hardware synchronization and spatial registration.

The authors trained AnyThermal, a thermal feature extractor obtained by knowledge distillation from a frozen DINOv2 RGB backbone using five RGB‑T datasets (ViVID++, STheReO, Freiburg, Boson Nighttime, and TartanRGBT), and evaluated it on three downstream tasks (cross‑modal place recognition, thermal segmentation, and monocular thermal depth estimation).

The authors report that AnyThermal combined with a SALAD visual place‑recognition head achieved 81.11% Recall@1 on the OdomBeyondVision indoor cross‑modal place‑recognition benchmark, surpassing the next best baseline by 5.2 percentage points.

The authors report that AnyThermal achieved a mean Intersection‑over‑Union of 53.47% on the MF‑Net thermal segmentation dataset, outperforming the frozen DINOv2 backbone (45.46% mIoU) and the ImageBind baseline (0.79% mIoU).

The authors report that AnyThermal achieved an absolute relative error of 0.0883 on monocular thermal depth estimation on the MS2 dataset, improving over the DINOv2 ViT‑B/14 backbone (0.0905) and the EfficientLite3 baseline (0.1015).

The authors demonstrate that adding the TartanRGBT dataset to the knowledge‑distillation pre‑training improves cross‑modal place‑recognition Recall@1 on the CART aerial benchmark from 25.98% (using only ViT‑B/14) to 29.49% (with AnyThermal), a relative gain of 13.5%.

The authors claim that the TartanRGBT platform provides hardware‑synchronized capture of two RGB and two thermal cameras at 30 Hz, with automatic start/stop via a single button and remote Wi‑Fi access to the onboard NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin computer.

The authors state that the TartanRGBT dataset is the first RGB‑T dataset offering both hardware synchronization and spatial registration across four diverse environment types, as shown by comparison with existing RGB‑T datasets in Table I.

The authors used the Spanish BERT model BETO combined with a Conditional Random Field layer for sequence labeling and achieved a trigger detection F1‑score of 0.94 on the ToxHabits test set when employing the full training dataset with sentence filtering and hyperparameter tuning.

The authors' binary BERT‑based sentence filtering model, fine‑tuned on BETO, discarded sentences lacking any potential triggers or arguments, thereby reducing the number of sentences processed in downstream stages.

The ToxHabits corpus comprises 1,499 Spanish clinical case reports, of which 1,199 documents (average 516 words per document) constitute the training set and 300 documents (average 498 words per document) constitute the test set.

The authors constructed an ensemble of six BERT‑CRF models trained with weighted random sampling on five‑fold cross‑validation subsets (80 % of the data) and one model trained on the full dataset for the full‑training‑dataset evaluation.

The authors jointly optimized trigger detection and argument extraction using a combined loss function L = α·L_tr + β·L_arg with equal weights α = β = 1, enabling shared representation learning in the multi‑output architecture.
The authors reported that the BERT‑Ensemble (full‑train) configuration achieved a precision of 0.97 for trigger detection on the official ToxHabits test set.
The authors reported that the BERT‑Ensemble (full‑train) configuration achieved a precision of 0.95 for argument detection on the official ToxHabits test set.

The authors observed that applying sentence filtering to the full‑training‑dataset increased the trigger detection F1‑score by 0.01 points (from 0.93 to 0.94) compared with the same model without filtering.

The study combined Sentinel-2 optical imagery, Sentinel-1 SAR data, and ERA5 meteorological reanalysis using Random Forest regression to estimate soil moisture at 10‑meter resolution across vegetated areas in Europe.

The study evaluated three Sentinel-1 orbit configurations (ascending, descending, both) and found that the descending orbit configuration consistently outperformed the ascending configuration, achieving an R² of 0.434 versus 0.404 for the same temporal matching condition.

The study applied a hybrid temporal matching strategy that paired same‑day Sentinel‑2 acquisitions with the nearest descending‑orbit Sentinel‑1 observation and achieved an R² of 0.514 (RMSE = 0.078 m³/m³) for soil moisture prediction.

The study tested ERA5 look‑back windows ranging from 0 to 20 days and identified that a 10‑day look‑back window yielded the highest performance (R² = 0.518, RMSE = 0.079 m³/m³) for the optimal Sentinel‑2 + Sentinel‑1 configuration.

The study extracted 768‑dimensional embeddings from the frozen encoder of the Prithvi 2.0 foundation model (300 M parameters) and compared them to hand‑crafted spectral indices, finding that the embeddings achieved an R² of 0.514, which was statistically indistinguishable from the hand‑crafted feature result of 0.514.

The study used 113 International Soil Moisture Network (ISMN) stations located in vegetated land cover types across Europe, with measurements collected from January 2019 to December 2024 at a depth of 5 cm.

The study calculated four vegetation and moisture indices from Sentinel‑2 Level‑2A data (NDVI, NDWI, NDMI, MSI) and incorporated first‑order temporal differences of all spectral features as input variables for the Random Forest models.

The study reported that multimodal fusion of Sentinel‑2 and Sentinel‑1 data improved the coefficient of determination by approximately 2 % (from R² = 0.507 for Sentinel‑2 alone to R² = 0.514 for the combined configuration) under the same spatial cross‑validation scheme.
